name: Perimortem Cesarean (Resuscitative Hysterotomy)
one_liner: Maternal cardiac arrest with fundus at or above the umbilicus: incise by minute 4 — delivery treats the mother.
indications: Maternal cardiac arrest with fundal height at or above the umbilicus (~>= 20 weeks)
No sustained ROSC within the first minutes of high-quality ACLS — begin preparing at arrest recognition, not after a failed code
contraindications: Fundus clearly below the umbilicus (uterus too small to cause aortocaval compression — hysterotomy will not aid the resuscitation)
Sustained ROSC (move to OR/definitive care instead — relative, reassess continuously)

time_target.label: Incision by minute 4 of arrest, delivery by minute 5 — but proceed even if the window has passed; survival is reported with later delivery and the mother still benefits.

team_calls: OB stat (do not wait for arrival)
Neonatal/NICU or pediatrics for the infant
Second RN as dedicated recorder (arrest, incision, delivery, ROSC times)
equipment: #10 scalpel — the only truly required instrument
Two cord clamps (or hemostats) + cord scissors
Lap pads / packing, suction if at hand
Antiseptic splash if immediately available (do not delay for prep)
Neonatal warmer, dry towels, infant BVM — set up in parallel

steps.detail: Arrest + fundus at/above umbilicus -> the decision is made. Start the clock; goal is incision by minute 4. Do not wait for OB, ultrasound, or fetal heart tones.

steps.action: CPR never stops; displace the uterus
steps.detail: High-quality compressions continue throughout the procedure. Manual LEFT uterine displacement (push uterus to patient's left) until the incision relieves it.

steps.action: Skip everything that delays the knife
steps.detail: No consent process, no sterile field, no transport to OR, no imaging. Splash antiseptic only if it is already in your hand.
Second provider/RN preps neonatal resuscitation (warmer, towels, BVM) while you cut. Call OB + NICU in parallel — they take over after, not before.
Xiphoid-to-pubis vertical midline through skin, fascia, and peritoneum — fastest and most forgiving for the non-obstetrician. Blood loss is minimal during arrest.

steps.action: Uterine incision with a finger guard
steps.detail: Vertical incision starting at the fundus. Once inside, lift the uterine wall and extend with scissors, using two fingers beneath as a guard against fetal injury.

steps.action: Deliver, clamp x2, cut, hand off
steps.detail: Deliver head or breech per lie, suction if needed, double-clamp and cut the cord, pass the infant to the neonatal team, keep the infant warm.
Pack the uterus/abdomen and continue ACLS — ROSC frequently follows delivery within minutes as aortocaval compression is relieved. After ROSC: oxytocin, antibiotics, OB to OR for closure.
Arrest, incision, delivery, ROSC. The recorder assigned in step 4 owns this.

meds.name: Epinephrine (ACLS)
meds.role: Standard ACLS — doses and defibrillation energies are UNCHANGED in pregnancy

adult.text: 1 mg IV/IO every 3-5 min per ACLS

notes: Place IV access ABOVE the diaphragm (aortocaval compression impairs femoral return)

meds.name: Oxytocin
meds.role: Uterine tone after delivery (give after ROSC, not during arrest)

adult.text: 10 units IM after delivery, or 20-40 units/L IV titrated. Avoid rapid IV bolus (hypotension).

cautions: Rapid undiluted IV bolus causes hypotension — IM or dilute infusion only

meds.name: Cefazolin
meds.role: Post-procedure antibiotic prophylaxis once ROSC achieved

pitfalls: Waiting — for OB, for ultrasound, for fetal heart tones, for consent. The procedure is part of the maternal resuscitation; delay kills both patients.
Deciding it is 'too late' after 5 minutes — intact survival is reported well beyond the window; the mother still benefits hemodynamically.
Low transverse skin incision by habit — vertical midline is faster and safer for the non-obstetrician.
Cutting the uterus without a finger guard — fetal lacerations.
No one assigned to the infant — two patients emerge from this procedure.
success_criteria: Incision started within ~4 min of arrest (or as soon as recognized)
Infant delivered and handed to a prepared neonatal team
Maternal ACLS uninterrupted; ROSC reassessed immediately after delivery
All four times documented (arrest, incision, delivery, ROSC)
aftercare: Post-ROSC bundle; OB to OR for uterine repair and closure
Oxytocin infusion for tone; watch for hemorrhage
Team debrief; complete documentation while times are fresh

references.label: AHA 2015 Scientific Statement
references.cite: Jeejeebhoy FM, et al. Cardiac Arrest in Pregnancy: A Scientific Statement From the American Heart Association. Circulation. 2015;132(18):1747-1773.

references.label: Rose 2015 — beyond the 4-minute rule
references.cite: Rose CH, et al. Challenging the 4- to 5-minute rule: from perimortem cesarean to resuscitative hysterotomy. Am J Obstet Gynecol. 2015;213(5):653-656.

references.label: Einav 2012 systematic review
references.cite: Einav S, Kaufman N, Sela HY. Maternal cardiac arrest and perimortem caesarean delivery: evidence or expert-based? Resuscitation. 2012;83(10):1191-1200.

references.label: Tintinalli 9e
references.cite: Tintinalli's Emergency Medicine: A Comprehensive Study Guide, 9th ed. Resuscitation in Pregnancy.

review.author: Claude (Fable 5), HALO T2 — pending physician review

drill.stem: A 32-year-old at approximately 34 weeks collapses in triage and is pulseless. CPR is in progress. You are the only physician in the department; OB is 25 minutes away. (Synthetic scenario.)

decision_points.prompt: Name the simultaneous first priorities as CPR starts.
decision_points.ideal: Continue high-quality ACLS, manual LEFT uterine displacement, call OB + neonatal + anesthesia, scalpel to the bedside, assign a recorder.

decision_points.prompt: When do you decide to perform the hysterotomy, and what triggers it?
decision_points.ideal: The decision is NOW, at arrest recognition: fundus at/above umbilicus + arrest. Goal incision by minute 4, delivery by minute 5 — do not wait for OB or imaging.

decision_points.prompt: Name two things you explicitly do NOT wait for.
decision_points.ideal: Ultrasound, fetal heart tones, consent, OB arrival, sterile prep, OR transport.

decision_points.prompt: Describe your abdominal and uterine incisions.
decision_points.ideal: Vertical midline skin incision xiphoid-to-pubis; vertical uterine incision from the fundus, extended with scissors over two fingers as a guard.

decision_points.prompt: The infant is out. Next two actions?
decision_points.ideal: Double-clamp and cut the cord, hand the infant to the prepared neonatal team (warm, dry, NRP) — and continue maternal ACLS.

decision_points.prompt: Why does delivery itself help the mother?
decision_points.ideal: It relieves aortocaval compression, restoring venous return and cardiac output — ROSC often follows within minutes of delivery.
